messages.content: revise this paragraph: The constitutionalists petitioned multiple times to shorten the preparation period and immediately convene the National Assembly; many constitutionalists even demanded the abolition of the autocratic monarchy and the adoption of parliamentary monarchy. The Qing court (the monarch) delayed convening the National Assembly on the grounds that “the promulgation of the constitution should precede the formation of the  National Assembly.” However, the development of the political situation then induced the petition movement for the opening of the parliament. The main reason was that the establishment of Provincial Assemblies provided legal forums of assembly for the discussion of constitutionalism, which greatly promoted the constitutional movement in the late Qing. There were three petitions in this movement. The first petition happened in the winter of the first year of the Xuantong era (1909), organized by Zhang Jian, chairman of the Provincial Assembly of Jiangsu, who gathered representatives from sixteen provinces to form the “Petition Federation of Provincial Assembly” (ziyiju qingyuan lianhehui, 咨议局请愿联合会) and went to Beijing to petition for the immediate convening of the National Assembly and the organization of a responsible cabinet. The Qing court ignored the petition, and tried to delay on the grounds of “nine years of preparation for the constitutionalism”. Therefore, the Petition Federation united with the “Association for the Establishment of the National Assembly” (guohui qichenghui, 国会期成会) in Beijing to organize the “Comrade Petition Union for the Establishment of the National Assembly”, and telegraphed an invitation to the Provincial Assemblies, chambers of commerce, education associations and overseas Chinese groups to send representatives to participate in the petition. The second petition took place in May of the second year of the Xuantong era (1910), but the Qing government stalled the issue with the same excuse. In September, the third petition was submitted. This time, the Qing court, to win back popular support, had to concede by shortening the preparatory period and issuing an edict to advance the convening of the National Assembly to the fifth year of the Xuantong era (1913). The Qing court strongly emphasized that the preparatory period was shortened at the request of all parties, and that “once it was announced, no further changes could be accepted.” The Qing court insisted that this was its last concession. At that time, Tang Hualong from Hubei and Tan Yankai from Hunan province still fought for convoking the National Assembly immediately and planned a fourth petition in Beijing, but they were suppressed by the Qing court and the petition movement died down thereafter. (Luo, 1974, pp. 157-166)

The constitutionalists made repeated petitions to shorten the preparation period and promptly call for the National Assembly. Some even demanded the abolition of the autocratic monarchy in favor of a parliamentary one. The Qing court, however, delayed convening the National Assembly, arguing that the constitution should be promulgated first. However, the formation of Provincial Assemblies provided legal forums for the discussion of constitutionalism, ultimately promoting the movement. There were three petitions. The first occurred in the winter of 1909, led by Zhang Jian, chairman of the Provincial Assembly of Jiangsu. The Qing court ignored the petition and delayed by citing the need for nine years of preparation for constitutionalism. The Petition Federation then united with the Association for the Establishment of the National Assembly to organize the Comrade Petition Union for the Establishment of the National Assembly, inviting various groups to participate. The second petition happened in May 1910, with the same outcome. The third petition was successful in advancing the convening of the National Assembly to the fifth year of the Xuantong era. Although some still fought for an immediate assembly, they were suppressed by the Qing court, and the petition movement waned. (Luo, 1974, pp. 157-166)

revise: This time, the Qing court, to win back popular support, had to concede by shortening the preparatory period and issuing an edict to advance the convening of the National Assembly to the fifth year of the Xuantong era (1913). The Qing court strongly emphasized that the preparatory period was shortened at the request of all parties, and that “once it was announced, no further changes could be accepted.” The Qing court insisted that this was its last concession. At that time, Tang Hualong from Hubei and Tan Yankai from Hunan province still fought for convoking the National Assembly immediately and planned a fourth petition in Beijing, but they were suppressed by the Qing court and the petition movement died down thereafter. (Luo, 1974, pp. 157-166)

To regain public support, the Qing court eventually conceded by shortening the preparatory period and issuing an edict to convene the National Assembly in the fifth year of the Xuantong era. The court made it clear that this was the last concession and that no further changes could be accepted. Despite this, Tang Hualong from Hubei and Tan Yankai from Hunan province still fought for an immediate convocation of the National Assembly and planned a fourth petition in Beijing. However, the Qing court suppressed their efforts, and the petition movement ultimately faded away. (Luo, 1974, pp. 157-166)

revise: Not long after the establishment of the Political Advisory Council, all the members passed the draft of the memorial requesting the immediate convention of the National Assembly, hopefully in the coming year. Soon, the Qing government issued an edict to shorten the nine-year preparation for constitutionalism to five years, and suppressed the on-going petition for the convocation of the National Assembly. The constitutionalists suffered a major setback in the Political Advisory Council and had disagreements with the Qing court. Some member of the Political Advisory Council boldly predicted that if the National Assembly was not promptly convoked, the country might perish before the end of the five-year period. Unfortunately, this prediction proved true. (Li, 2011, p. 141).

Shortly after the establishment of the Political Advisory Council, all members passed a draft memorial requesting the prompt convening of the National Assembly, preferably within the next year. The Qing government soon issued an edict shortening the preparation period for constitutionalism from nine to five years and suppressed the ongoing petition for the assembly's convocation. This setback caused disagreements between the constitutionalists and the Qing court, with some council members boldly predicting that without an immediate convocation of the National Assembly, the country might perish before the end of the five-year period. Sadly, their prediction proved to be accurate. (Li, 2011, p. 141)

revise: Before the Qing government formally issued the edict on the petition for the immediate convening of the National Assembly, there were rumors that the National Assembly would not be convoked immediately. On September 29, 1910, Venerable Prince Yulang, a Grand Minister of State, went to the Political Advisory Council to discuss the issue of the establishment of the National Assembly. During the discussion, assemblymen Shao Xi and Yi Zongkui, regarding the aforementioned edict, brought forward in advance the countersigning responsibility of Grand Councilors for the first time: “It is a common practice in the Eastern and Western countries that all the decrees of the monarch are countersigned by the Ministers of State, which, for one thing, serves as a sign of administrative responsibility on behalf of the monarch, and for another, accountability to the National Assembly.

Rumors circulated before the Qing government formally issued an edict regarding the immediate convocation of the National Assembly, casting doubt on the assembly's prompt establishment. On September 29, 1910, Venerable Prince Yulang, a Grand Minister of State, visited the Political Advisory Council to discuss the matter. During their discussion, assemblymen Shao Xi and Yi Zongkui raised the countersigning responsibility of Grand Councilors for the first time, noting that it was a common practice in Eastern and Western countries. The countersignature served as a sign of administrative responsibility on behalf of the monarch and accountability to the National Assembly.

revise: It is a common practice in the Eastern and Western countries that all the decrees of the monarch are countersigned by the Ministers of State, which, for one thing, serves as a sign of administrative responsibility on behalf of the monarch, and for another, accountability to the National Assembly. All the imperial edicts in China today have been countersigned by Grand Ministers of State. With the National Assembly not yet convened while the Political Advisory Council was established, is the purpose of the countersignature the same as that in other countries, i.e., responsibility for the monarch and accountability to the Political Advisory Council? Is there any difference at all from the countersignatures of the state ministers in other countries?”

In both Eastern and Western countries, it is customary for all decrees of the monarch to be countersigned by the Ministers of State. This serves two purposes: to demonstrate administrative responsibility on behalf of the monarch and to establish accountability to the National Assembly. In present-day China, all imperial edicts are countersigned by Grand Ministers of State. However, given that the National Assembly had yet to convene when the Political Advisory Council was established, questions arose about the purpose of these countersignatures. Specifically, were they intended to establish responsibility for the monarch and accountability to the Political Advisory Council, or was there no significant difference from the countersignatures of state ministers in other countries?

revise: After the imperial edict is issued, if the National Assembly can be convened next year, it will meet the requirement of the people of the whole country if it can be convened next year; otherwise, the Grand Ministers of Statethe Grand Councilors will be held responsible for the countersignature, that is, they do not need to countersign anymore

After an imperial edict is issued, it will meet the people's expectations if the National Assembly can be convoked the following year. However, if the assembly cannot be established within that timeline, then the Grand Ministers of State, and consequently the Grand Councilors, will be held accountable for the countersignature. In such a scenario, there may be no need for further countersignatures.

revise: After the edict was issued, according to some assemblyman: “A few days ago, the motion concerning the National Assembly has been voted on in the Political Advisory Council. In a constitutional country, a motion, after being voted on, is to be approved by the emperor, wherein the so-called approval is only nominal as the resolutions of the House of Representatives are always passed … Now the decision of the princes and grand ministers to convene the National Assembly on the fourth year vetoed the Political Advisory Council’s resolution on the immediate convocation of the National Assembly next year. How would it be possible for the Political Advisory Council to lay the foundation for and nurture the spirit of the National Assembly?

After the imperial edict was issued, some assemblymen remarked: "Recently, the motion concerning the National Assembly was voted on in the Political Advisory Council. In a constitutional country, once such a motion is passed, it should be approved by the emperor. However, in such cases, the emperor's approval is typically nominal, as is the case with the resolutions passed in the House of Representatives. Now, with the princes and grand ministers deciding to convene the National Assembly in the fourth year, they have effectively vetoed the Political Advisory Council's resolution for the immediate convocation of the assembly next year. How can the Political Advisory Council lay the foundation for and nurture the spirit of the National Assembly, given these developments?"

revise: Unable to directly criticize the emperor and the regent, the council members could only point their finger at the princes and grand ministers of the Bureau of Government Affairs who countersigned the imperial edicts, and the “several petty officials” of the Commission for Studying Constitutional Government, accusing them of ignoring public opinion, hijacking the emperor and the regent, and going against the trend. According to them, the princes and grand ministers shall be held responsible: “This is the request agreed upon by provincial governors and the people, and only a handful of princes and grand ministers disagree … However, ever since the preparation for constitutionalism, with the emperor still young and the regent accepting the voice of the many, all the legislation was entrusted to the princes and grand ministers of the Commission for Studying Constitutional Government, where only several petty officials were responsible for the compilation of laws … The Speaker of the Political Advisory Council should consult with the Commission for Studying Constitutional Government, and promptly submit the drafts of constitution, parliament acts, and election law to the Political Advisory Council for approval. It is better to rely on the opinion of the majority than on that of the minority … My Majesty and the regent had held no prejudices at all ... But in the Chinese form of government, whenever a special matter occurs, it must be discussed with the princes and grand ministers of the Bureau of Government Affairs. Since they have signed their names, they should be held responsible for their countersignatures ... The emperor and the regent, fully aware of the rapidly-changing situation and the pressing crisis, intended to open the parliament immediately, while the princes and grand ministers of the Bureau of Government Affairs insisted on the five-year arrangement, which made the immediate convention an impossible mission. The princes and grand ministers who countersign the edicts shall be held responsible for all the internal and external crises thereafter”

Unable to openly criticize the emperor and the regent, council members directed their criticism towards the princes and grand ministers of the Bureau of Government Affairs, who countersigned the imperial edicts, and the "several petty officials" of the Commission for Studying Constitutional Government. The members accused them of disregarding public opinion, hijacking the emperor and regent, and going against the trend. They argued that the princes and grand ministers should be held accountable, stating that this was a request agreed upon by provincial governors and the people, and only a handful of princes and grand ministers were opposed. 

Since the preparation for constitutionalism, all legislation had been entrusted to the princes and grand ministers of the Commission for Studying Constitutional Government, where only a few petty officials were responsible for compiling laws. The speaker of the Political Advisory Council should consult with this commission and promptly submit drafts of the constitution, parliament acts, and election laws to the council for approval. The majority opinion should carry greater weight than that of the minority. 

While the emperor and the regent held no prejudices, special matters in the Chinese form of government required discussion with the princes and grand ministers of the Bureau of Government Affairs. These officials should be held responsible for their countersignatures. The emperor and regent intended to open the parliament immediately, but the princes and grand ministers of the bureau insisted on the five-year arrangement, making immediate convocation impossible. As a result, the princes and grand ministers who countersigned the edicts should be held responsible for all subsequent internal and external crises.

revise: This time, the council members did not find fault with the Grand Council, but with the Bureau of Government Affairs and the Commission for Studying Constitutional Government. However, most of the ministers of the Bureau of Government Affairs were concurrently the Grand Councilors, who also take charge of the Commission for Studying Constitutional Government. Due to their overlap and disputes in legislative functions, the Commission for Studying Constitutional Government became the target of attack by the Political Advisory Council.

This time, the council members did not criticize the Grand Council, but instead directed their attention towards the Bureau of Government Affairs and the Commission for Studying Constitutional Government. However, the majority of the ministers of the Bureau of Government Affairs also held concurrent positions as Grand Councilors, and therefore, were also responsible for the Commission for Studying Constitutional Government. Because of this overlap and disputes over legislative functions, the Commission for Studying Constitutional Government became a target of attack by the Political Advisory Council.

revise: We may conclude this chapter with a calm and professional speech from Political Advisory Council member Wang Rongbao: “As members working and speaking at the Political Advisory Council, we need to know, first its duties and authorities, and second its status. According to the Charter of Political Advisory Council, it has the right to review and handle provincial governors’ infringement on the authority of Provincial Assemblies and violation of laws. According to the Charter, the word “review and handle” can be divided into three stages from the perspective of nomology, reviewing, resolution, and memorializing to the throne … As to what the imperial court will do with the governors’ infringement and offences after the memorialization to the throne, whether to punish them or not is the power of the emperor and it is not within the scope of the Political Advisory Council’s inspection. There is no room for the Political Advisory Council to intervene either … To put it simply, punishment is one thing while inspection is another. Punishment or not is subject to the imperial court while proper inspection is the responsibility of the Political Advisory Council. In fulfilling this duty, the Political Advisory Council must have four perceptions. First, abiding by the Charter of Political Advisory Council. Second, clarifying the scope of its authority. Third, playing fair. Fourth, having an indomitable resolve. Only with these four qualities can the Political Advisory Council become a true mouthpiece of public opinion and have a sound legal thinking. Otherwise, it would have no grounds to advocate the law or accuse others of violating the law. Has it fully realized its own legal status? Does it have firm legal grounds?” (Li, 2011, p. 195).

This chapter concludes with an eloquent and composed speech by Political Advisory Council member Wang Rongbao: "As members of the Political Advisory Council, we must understand its duties, authorities, and status. According to the council's charter, it has the right to review and handle cases of provincial governors infringing upon the authority of Provincial Assemblies and violating laws. The word 'review and handle' can be divided into three stages of nomology: reviewing, resolution, and memorializing to the throne. However, what the imperial court chooses to do with the governors' offenses after the memorialization to the throne, whether to punish them or not, is the emperor's prerogative and not within the council's purview or intervention. Punishment is one thing; inspection is another. The emperor and his court have the power to punish, while the council has the responsibility to inspect. In fulfilling this duty, the council must have four perceptions: abiding by its charter, clarifying the scope of its authority, playing fairly, and having an unyielding resolve. Only with these qualities can the council become a true representative of public opinion and have a sound legal thinking. Otherwise, it would have no grounds to advocate for or accuse others of violating the law. Does the council fully recognize its legal status? Does it have a firm legal foundation?" (Li, 2011, p. 195).
